topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the blunt finality of a closing door. Professor Albright was moving to a different institution. My stomach plummeted, a cold, heavy sensation that made my hands tremble as I reread the subject line. All the late nights, the meticulously cited pages, the agonizing revisions—it all felt suddenly pointless. I stared at the screen, the words blurring into meaningless symbols. I picked up my pen, the plastic cool against my fingertips, and found I couldn't even form a coherent sentence. Just a small, defeated sigh escaped my lips, a sound swallowed instantly by the quiet of my cramped dorm room. The fluorescent light above flickered, mirroring the unstable state of my own composure.

[story 2]

"He's gone," Sarah murmured, not looking up from her textbook, her voice flat. I felt a strange numbness spread through my chest, like a slow-moving fog. I traced the condensation ring left by my water bottle on the desk, the outline of the circle mocking the circular, endless loop of my own inadequacy. Every achievement felt tainted now, every successful citation a hollow echo. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor. My legs felt unsteady, and I walked to the window, watching the bustling campus life below. The energy of others seemed vibrant, effortless, while I felt anchored to the floor, heavy and immovable.

[story 3]

I kept retyping the opening paragraph of my thesis, deleting and rewriting the same sentence repeatedly. Each revision was a small, futile gesture against the enormity of the situation. When the news finally registered, it wasn't a sharp shock, but a dull, pervasive ache, like a bruise that wouldn't fade. I leaned back in my chair, letting my head fall against the cool plastic of the backrest. My gaze drifted to the stack of annotated papers beside me; they seemed to mock my inability to grasp the subject matter, my failure to produce anything substantial. A deep, weary slump settled into my shoulders, making the simple act of breathing feel like an exhausting physical strain.
